summary:
Alpha (α)-fibers are highly conducting with speeds up to about 120 meters/second; they represent one type among several categories distinguished primarily based upon their rate and efficiency of impulse transmission through neural pathways at varying rates from slowest unmyelinated C-fibers (~3m/sec), intermediate B & D-fibers (<50m/sec for both), faster than our answer but still slower than A-alpha ones like A-beta (\~68+). Option 'A' accurately describes these properties making it correct here as well since no specific range was asked just "speed". To directly address your request while keeping within two concise statements:

The primary focus is on rapid electrical signal propagation via alpha (α-)fibre channels which can transmit signals quickly—typically reaching peak velocities near or above ~90 metres per second. This makes them distinctively fast amongst nerve fibre classifications relative to others such as beta/beta-delta variants whose maxima fall below those values yet remain significantly swifter overall versus non-myelin coated varieties including thinly insulated classes down into lower single digit tens-of-meters-per-second regimes typical even then only under optimal conditions without
